flashcards.front: What is a file stream in C++ and what are the three main types?
flashcards.back: In C++, a file stream is an object that provides an interface to read from or write to files. The three main types are ifstream (input), ofstream (output), and fstream (both input and output).

flashcards.front: Explain the difference between text mode and binary mode when opening a file.
flashcards.back: Text mode interprets bytes as characters and may convert newline characters (e.g., \n to \r\n on Windows). Binary mode reads raw bytes without any conversion, preserving exact data layout.

flashcards.front: How do you open a file for reading and writing using fstream in C++?
flashcards.back: Declare an fstream object, call open("filename", ios::in|ios::out|ios::binary) to open for both reading and writing in binary mode. Always check if the file opened successfully using fail().

flashcards.front: What method can you use to check if a file was opened successfully?
flashcards.back: Use the fail() member function of the stream object, which returns true if the most recent stream operation failed. You can also use is_open() to check if the file is currently open.

flashcards.front: Explain the purpose of the fail, bad, and eof state bits in file streams.
flashcards.back: The fail bit is set when a logical error occurs (e.g., opening a non-existent file). The bad bit indicates a fatal stream error (e.g., loss of integrity). The eof bit is set when the end of the file is reached during reading.

flashcards.front: How does random access work in file streams using seekg and seekp?
flashcards.back: seekg(offset, reference) moves the get pointer (reading) to a position relative to ios::beg, ios::cur, or ios::end. seekp(offset, reference) moves the put pointer (writing) similarly, allowing random access to any byte in the file.

flashcards.front: How do you read and write binary data using member functions read and write?
flashcards.back: Use stream.read(char* buffer, size_t count) to read count bytes into buffer, and stream.write(const char* buffer, size_t count) to write count bytes from buffer. Cast the buffer to char* when using these functions.

flashcards.front: Why is it important to close a file and how is it done in C++?
flashcards.back: Closing a file flushes any buffered data to the physical file and releases system resources. Call the member function close() on the stream object, or let the destructor automatically close the file when the object goes out of scope.
